description: Centralized dialogue system for Legend of Silicon Valley — 30 NPCs across 10 buildings

lines: Every empire starts with a napkin sketch.
Level 1: Product Ideation.
Validate your idea in 3 rounds.
The Valley rewards the bold. You ready?

choices.text: Who else should I talk to?

lines: Solve a problem you've felt yourself.
Talk to 20 strangers before writing code.
If nobody says 'shut up and take my money'...
...you haven't found the pain yet.
Alex down at the Garage builds MVPs overnight.
And Priya here can validate your assumptions.
Don't skip validation. Trust me.
Hi! I'm Priya. I do user research.
Gut feelings are nice. Data is better.
Want me to stress-test your idea?
Step 1: Define your riskiest assumption.
Step 2: Design a test that could kill it.
Step 3: Run it with real humans, not friends.
If it survives, you might have something.
Good. Now go build something tangible.
Alex at the Garage ships fast.
Tell him Priya sent you.

choices.hint: The Garage is south of here.

lines: Three startups. Three failures. One lesson.
Cash. Is. King.
Don't look at me like that. Sit down.
First startup: burned $2M on office furniture.
Second: hired 40 people before finding PMF.
Third: ran out of runway 2 weeks before profit.
Two weeks. That one still stings.
Once pitched a VC while my servers were on fire.
Literally. The rack caught fire in the colo.
Got the term sheet though. He respected hustle.
Lost the company 6 months later anyway. Ha.
Find Diana at the Board Room. CFO type.
She almost saved my third company.
Tell her I sent you. She'll understand.

choices.hint: The Board Room is far east, past the Conference.

lines: Welcome to the Garage. No slides here.
Level 2: MVP Development.
Ship something real in 3 sprints.
Code talks. Everything else walks.
Build the smallest thing that proves value.
If it takes more than 2 weeks, scope is wrong.
Ship ugly. Ship broken. Ship FAST.
Users forgive bugs. They won't forgive vapor.
Once you've shipped, talk to Jordan.
He's at the Accelerator. Knows every VC.
Also, Dani here handles DevOps. Ship safer.

choices.hint: The Accelerator is west of the Garage.

lines: 99.99% uptime or I don't sleep.
I'm Dani. I keep things running.
Your deploy pipeline scares me already.

choices.text: Know anyone at the Data Center?

lines: Automate deploys. No manual SSH. Ever.
Monitor everything. Alert on the right things.
Rollbacks should take 30 seconds, not 30 minutes.
If you can't deploy on Friday, your CI is broken.
When you outgrow this garage setup...
Find SRE Pat at the Data Center.
Pat's paranoid, but that's the job.

choices.hint: The Data Center is east, past the Accelerator.

lines: Hi! I'm interning here this summer!
So like... what IS a startup exactly?
Is it just a company that loses money on purpose?
Wait, so you raise money to NOT be profitable?
And VCs want 10x returns in 7 years?
What happens to the 9 out of 10 that fail?
This sounds like a video game with permadeath.
Oh, a reporter wants MY opinion?
Quote me: 'I thought the ping pong table WAS the product.'
Wait, don't actually print that. Or do. Whatever.
Jordan here. I know a guy who knows a guy.
Level 3: Fundraising.
Convince investors your vision is worth millions.
Let's get you that seed round.
VCs invest in teams, not decks.
Know your TAM, SAM, SOM cold.
Ask for intros, not cold emails.
Best fundraise: when you don't need the money.
Casey at the Loft is building culture.
You'll need a team before Series A.
Tell her Jordan sent you. She owes me.

choices.hint: The Loft is north of the Accelerator.

lines: STOP. Your posture is terrible.
I'm the Demo Coach. I fix pitches.
Demo Day is everything. Are you ready? No.

choices.text: Who else should I prep with?

lines: First 10 seconds: hook them or lose them.
Tell a story. Numbers alone don't fundraise.
End with the ask. Always end with the ask.
Practice until it feels like breathing.
Talk to the Fellow Founder here.
Nothing sharpens a pitch like a rival.
Friendly competition. Emphasis on competition.
Oh hey. Another founder in the cohort.
How's your MRR? Mine's... growing. Definitely.
We're all in this together. Sort of.
We just hit 10K users. No big deal.
Okay fine, 8K are bots. But still.
What about you? Don't lie, I'll check.
Real talk? This is the loneliest job.
Everyone posts wins. Nobody posts 3am doubts.
Find one founder you can be honest with.
That's worth more than any VC check.
Corporate called. I hung up.
Level 4: Team Building.
Your first 10 hires define everything.
Let's build something that doesn't suck.
Culture isn't a ping pong table.
It's how you treat people at 2am on a deadline.
Hire slow. Fire fast. Sounds brutal.
But one toxic hire poisons the whole well.
Morgan at the Conference does growth.
She's intense but she gets results.
Also talk to Riya here. Best recruiter around.

choices.hint: The Conference is east of the Loft.

lines: Riya here. I find the unfindable.
Every founder says 'we only hire A players.'
Nobody knows what that means. I do.
Hire for slope, not y-intercept.
Best engineers want hard problems, not perks.
Your job post is boring. Rewrite it.
Referrals beat job boards 10 to 1.
Talk to Kai here. Best designer I know.
Also, the Data Center is hiring SREs.
Pat over there is solid. Paranoid, but solid.

choices.hint: The Data Center is east of here.

lines: If your onboarding takes more than 3 taps...
...you've already lost.
I'm Kai. I design things that don't suck.
Design is not decoration. It's communication.
Every pixel should earn its place.
Dark patterns get short-term metrics.
They also get long-term hatred. Choose wisely.
The Growth Hacker at the Conference...
...let's just say we disagree on ethics.
Talk to them. But question everything they say.

choices.hint: The Conference is east of the Loft.

lines: Metrics don't lie. People do.
Level 5: Go-to-Market Strategy.
Scale from thousands to millions.
Let's make your hockey stick real.
Know your CAC and LTV. Tattoo them on your arm.
The best channel is the one competitors ignore.
Viral loops beat paid ads at scale.
Product-led growth: make the product sell itself.
You'll need infrastructure to handle scale.
The Data Center team handles that.
Also, Zoe the journalist is here. Be careful.

choices.hint: The Data Center is east of the Conference.

lines: A/B test EVERYTHING. Even this conversation.
I'm the Growth Hacker. Results are all that matter.
Ethics? That's a Series B problem.

choices.text: What does Kai think of you?

lines: Scrape competitor reviews for pain points.
Gamify onboarding. Dopamine is free.
Send push notifications at 9am. Trust me.
If the DAU chart isn't up, nothing else matters.
Kai? The designer? Loves to lecture me.
Says my tactics are 'dark patterns.'
I say dark patterns are just patterns that work.
We agree to disagree. Loudly.

choices.text: You two should talk it out

lines: A reporter? Finally, some earned media.
Quote me: 'Growth isn't a department, it's a religion.'
Make sure you spell my LinkedIn handle right.
Zoe Chen, TechCrunch. Don't worry, I'm off duty.
Actually that's a lie. I'm never off duty.
So... tell me about your startup.

choices.text: What do you want to know?

lines: What problem are you actually solving?
And don't say 'disrupting.' I beg you.
How's your runway? Burn rate?
Relax. This is off the record. Mostly.

choices.text: I have a story for you

lines: I'm writing a piece on the startup ecosystem.
I need color. Quotes from real people.
Get me quotes from the Intern, the Retail Investor...
...the Growth Hacker, and the ML Engineer.
Four quotes. Then we'll talk front page.
You got all four? Impressive hustle.
This is going to be a great piece.
I'll make sure your startup gets a mention.
Off the record: you're going to make it.
Welcome to the humming heart of the Valley.
Level 6: Scaling Infrastructure.
Your code works on localhost. Cute.
Now make it work for a million users.
Cache everything. Database calls are expensive.
Horizontal scaling beats vertical every time.
Your bottleneck is never where you think it is.
Profile first. Optimize second. Guess never.
Infrastructure is solved. Now survive the board.
Head to the Board Room. They're... intense.
Pat and the ML Engineer here can help too.

choices.hint: The Board Room is east of the Data Center.

lines: Shh. I'm watching the dashboards.
I'm Pat. Site Reliability. The pager never stops.
Last outage cost us $50K per minute. Per. Minute.
Worst outage: intern ran DROP TABLE in prod.
Second worst: DNS expired on a Saturday.
Best practice: everything fails. Plan for it.
I sleep with my pager. My partner hates it.
The ML Engineer is here too.
Fair warning: they want to add AI to everything.
Last week they pitched an ML-powered thermostat.
For the server room. We already have one. It works.

choices.text: I have to meet this person

lines: Have you considered adding ML to your product?
No? What about AI? LLMs? RAG pipelines?
Your sandwich shop app NEEDS a recommendation engine.
Picture this: AI-powered user onboarding.
ML-driven push notification timing.
A neural net that predicts churn before signup.
Yes, I said BEFORE. That's the innovation.

choices.text: That's not how any of this works

lines: A journalist? Perfect. Let me explain AGI.
Quote: 'Every startup is an AI company now.'
'They just don't know it yet.'
Make sure to mention my GitHub stars.
Take a seat. This won't be comfortable.
Level 7: Crisis Management.
Every startup hits a wall. How you respond matters.
Show me you can lead under pressure.
Bad news doesn't age well. Deliver it fast.
Never lie to your board. We always find out.
Have a plan before you report the problem.
The best founders grow the most during crises.
Survive the board, then raise more capital.
The VC Partner at the Venture office is next.
Diana here can prep your financials first.

choices.hint: The Venture office is east of the Board Room.

lines: Diana. CFO. I know your burn rate better than you.
Let me guess: 18 months of runway?
Wrong. It's 14. You forgot the tax bill.

choices.text: Someone named Old Timer sent me
Where should I go for IP stuff?

lines: Unit economics. Say it with me. Unit. Economics.
If CAC exceeds LTV, you're buying users at a loss.
Gross margin above 70% or VCs won't look twice.
Cash flow positive beats 'we'll figure it out later.'
He sent you? That stubborn old fool.
I almost saved his third company. Almost.
Two more weeks of runway and we'd have made it.
He taught me: never let the cash hit zero.
Now I make sure it never does. For anyone.
Protect your IP before you scale.
The Patent Attorney at the Law office is sharp.
Tell them Diana sent you. They owe me a favor.

choices.hint: The Law office is east of the Venture building.

lines: Perception IS reality. I manage both.
I'm your PR advisor. Let's craft the narrative.
Every crisis is a rebranding opportunity.
Step 1: Acknowledge. Never deny or deflect.
Step 2: Apologize without qualifiers.
Step 3: Action plan. What are you fixing?
Step 4: Follow up. Show you actually did it.
Zoe Chen at the Conference? She's sharp.
Never say 'no comment.' Say 'here's what I can share.'
Control the narrative or it controls you.
And for the love of god, stay off Twitter during a crisis.
I've seen 10,000 pitches. Yours has 60 seconds.
Level 8: Series A Fundraising.
Prove you can build a billion-dollar company.
Impress me.
Show me a moat. Network effects. Switching costs.
TAM better be $10B+. I don't do lifestyle businesses.
Why you? Why now? Why this team?
Show traction. Revenue beats projections.
After funding, protect your company legally.
The Law office has the attorneys you need.
My associate here can brief you on due diligence.

choices.hint: The Law office is east of the Venture building.

lines: *yawns* Sorry. Due diligence until 3am.
I'm the associate. I do the actual work.
The partner takes the meetings. I read the code.
Honestly? We pass on 99% of deals.
Most pitches blur together after the first thousand.
The ones that stand out? Founders who've felt the pain.
Not 'I read a report.' Actual lived experience.
Between us? Try the Angel Investor here.
Angels write smaller checks but ask fewer questions.
They've actually built companies before.
VCs... mostly read about building companies.

choices.text: Don't let your boss hear that

lines: Hey! I write checks on napkins. Literally.
Had a good exit in '09. Now I give back.
What's your story?
I invest in people, not spreadsheets.
$25K to $100K. Keep it simple. SAFE note.
No board seat. No weekly reports.
Just build something great and keep me posted.
Old Timer... you mean Dave? That's my old co-founder.
We built our first startup in a garage. Like this one.
He blames himself for the failures. Shouldn't.
The market killed us. Not the team.
Tell him I said hi. And that I'd build with him again.
The fine print is where fortunes are made or lost.
Level 9: Legal & Compliance.
Incorporate right. Protect your IP. Don't get sued.
Let's make sure you're bulletproof.

choices.text: I'll get my docs in order first

lines: Delaware C-Corp. Don't even think about LLC.
Vesting schedules: 4 years, 1-year cliff. Standard.
Get your 83(b) election filed in 30 days.
Miss that deadline and the IRS wins.
Last stop: NASDAQ. The endgame.
The trading floor awaits.
My patent colleague and Lee here can help prep.

choices.hint: NASDAQ is the final building, far east.

lines: Is that algorithm patentable? Probably.
Is that button color trademarkable? Also probably.
I'm the Patent Attorney. Everything is IP.
File provisional patents early. $300 buys you 12 months.
Trade secrets are free but fragile.
Open source your commodity code. Patent your moat.
And never, EVER discuss IP without an NDA.
Lee the Paralegal handles the filings. Talk to them.
Also, the Angel Investor at Venture funds IP-heavy startups.
Tell them I flagged your tech as defensible.

choices.hint: The Venture office is west of Law.

lines: Lee. Paralegal. I do the actual work here.
Attorneys bill $800/hr. I bill $150 and work faster.
What do you need filed?
Use Clerky or Stripe Atlas to incorporate. Not a lawyer.
Standard SAFE notes don't need custom legal review.
Save the $50K legal bill for when you actually need it.
That's usually your first lawsuit. It'll come.
If you're thinking IPO, talk to the advisor at NASDAQ.
They know the SEC filing process inside and out.
It's the big leagues over there. You ready?

choices.hint: NASDAQ is the final building, far east.

lines: The bell rings at 9:30. Sharp.
Level 10: IPO Day.
Price it right. Ring the bell. Don't throw up.
This is what it all comes down to.
Markets are driven by fear and greed. That's it.
Your stock price on day one means nothing.
What matters: revenue growth, quarter over quarter.
Wall Street has the memory of a goldfish.
You started with a napkin sketch at home.
Now you're ringing the NASDAQ bell.
Most people dream about this. You built it.
Remember everyone who helped you get here.
I speak in multiples and basis points.
IPO advisor. I've taken 47 companies public.
The question is: should YOU go public?
IPO: maximum valuation, maximum headache.
Acquisition: quick exit, someone else's vision.
Stay private: keep control, limit liquidity.
There's no wrong answer. Only trade-offs.
47 IPOs. Know what they all have in common?
The founders who made it weren't the smartest.
They were the most stubborn.
Keep going. That's the whole secret.
TO. THE. MOON.
Diamond hands baby! I'm never selling!
I bought at the top and I'd do it again!
Okay real talk? Beneath the memes...
Retail investors believe in what they buy.
We're not just customers. We're evangelists.
A million small believers beat one big VC.
A reporter? FINALLY someone covers retail!
Quote: 'I YOLO'd my savings and I'm UP 2%!'
'That's after being down 80% but we don't talk about that.'
Print it all. The people need to hear this.
Welcome to The Legend of Silicon Valley!
Your entrepreneurial journey begins now.
Explore the Valley. Talk to everyone.
You've entered a new building.
Look around and talk to people.
You've returned to the main world.
Continue your journey.
Use arrow keys or WASD to move.
Walk up to NPCs to start conversations.
Press + or - to zoom in and out.
Get close to NPCs to trigger dialogue.
Dialogues advance automatically.
Move away to end conversations.

founding_story.description: Main quest: from napkin sketch to IPO bell

mentor_network.description: Side quest: the Old Timer's redemption arc

valley_gossip.description: Side quest: help Journalist Zoe collect press quotes

lines: Wow, your user numbers are impressive!
You're really gaining traction.
Keep up the great work!
Your revenue growth is fantastic!
You're building a sustainable business.
Time to think about scaling!
Every startup starts somewhere.
Focus on finding product-market fit.
Don't give up, keep iterating!

motivational: The best time to plant a tree was 20 years ago. The second best time is now.
Innovation distinguishes between a leader and a follower.
Great things never come from comfort zones.
Dream it. Wish it. Do it.
tech_quotes: The future is already here, it's just not evenly distributed.
Any sufficiently advanced technology is indistinguishable from magic.
business_wisdom: Cash flow is the lifeblood of business.
Customer acquisition cost must be less than lifetime value.
Execution is more important than ideas.
